sentences.text: Olga Kovacs.
K-O-V-A-C-S.
That's the Hungarian spelling.
No, I have not.
I was born in Salgotarjan, Hungary, on May 23, 1926.
S-A-L-G-O-T-A-R-J-A-N.
We were a family of 14.
My parents, my grandparents, paternal grandparents, aunts, uncles, and cousins.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have never known my maternal grandmother, and my grandfather-- paternal grandmother-- and my paternal grandfather passed away when I was a toddler.
We always lived in Salgotarjan and my parents grew up in the town.
I started schooling in Salgotarjan where my parents had a business.
The grandparents were [?
Cecilia ?] and [?
Jacob ?] Biheller.
B-I-H-E-L-L-E-R.
My paternal grandparents were [?
Louise ?] and [?
Leopold ?] Kohn.
K-O-H-N.
My parents-- my mother's first name is Sari-- S-A-R-I-- and my father's first name is Dezso-- D-E-Z-S-O.
Kovacs.
Yes, I tell you why.
When the brothers-- father had two brothers-- went to college, then as far as I remember, being told by my father, the dean suggested to my grandfather that Mr.
Kohn-- it would be a good idea if the children take up a Hungarian name.
So the Kohn-- K-O-H-N-- thus became Kovacs, which in English means Smith.
Father had two brothers.
Uncle [?
Bela ?] and [?
Nandor. ?] Both were married.
Eventually Uncle [?
Nandor ?] divorced.
He had a son-- my cousin George-- who died a number of years ago in Spain.
And Uncle [?
Bela's ?] wife-- Mucu-- M-U-C-U-- lives in Budapest with my cousin, Vera, her daughter, Susan, and two grandchildren, Gabor and Aniko-- A-N-I-K-O.
My father's two brothers.
My mother's two brothers were [?
Bela, ?] who was married to Irene.
They had a daughter, Edith.
The other brother was Gino, married to Lilly and had a daughter, Agnes.
Mother's sister Olga was married to Dr.
Leslie-- or Laslow, Hungarian-- Laslow Schwabach.
S-C-H-W-A-B-A-C-H.
We all lived in Salgotarjan, yes.
It was a most happy childhood filled with-- [CRYING] I'm sorry-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] filled with love and devotion.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was beautiful.
Oh, sure.
Oh, sure.
Salgotarjan was a small town, so we had constant contact with each other.
And we just adored each other.
There was no dissension.
And I remember, when-- I-- I hear this in America, that when there are more than two or three grandchildren there are sometimes favorites.
I never felt that there are favorites.
We were-- we were devoted to each other.
And-- and the grandparents were devoted to us.
And it was a most happy environment.
Never.
So did-- were you very close to your cousins?
Yes.
Yes, very good friends.
My grandfather had an undertaking, it was called Salgo Liquor and Rum Factory.
This is spelled S-A-L-G-O.
And there belonged wine and alcohol wholesale business, a wine vinegar manufacturing plant, some land, and liquor and-- distillery, what you really call in English.
My grandfather-- my maternal grandfather-- had a hardware business, which upon their retirement-- the grandparents' retirement-- went to the two brothers.
The sisters by then were married already.
And then at the other hand, my paternal grandfather, upon his retirement, of course, left the business to my father and his two brothers.
OK.
And so how would you describe your status, economically and socially, in the town?
We-- we did not have any-- any-- any problems financially.
No-- no problems.
Yes.
Several thousand Jewish people in town.
Salgotarjan is about 70 to 73 miles northeast from Budapest.
Kilometers, kilometers.
Close to the Czechoslovak border of 1939.
Very close, just a couple of kilometers from the border.
Yes.
I believe my maternal grandparents' grandparents came from northern Czechoslovakia, but everybody was born in Salgotarjan.
Hungarian, and the grandfather's house sometimes we spoke German, with a little Yiddish.
A little bit of Yiddish.
Little bit of Yiddish.
Yes.

Well, it was an orthodox congregation, but in the strict sense, because, for example, when we went to the synagogue the women were sitting upstairs behind the-- not the bars, how do you call this in English?
Behind the balustrade.
Probably.
And the men were downstairs.
So there-- there-- there was also a segment of what you would call here Reformed Jewish entity, and the Orthodox entity.
One--
One congregation, yes.
Father-- every day, father was putting on the [NON-ENGLISH].
We had mezuzah on every door.
We had a strict kosher household, and we duly observed Pesach.
On the high holy days the business was closed, and father, along with his brothers, were praying in kittel.
On Fridays-- on Saturdays our business was open.
Grandfather's business closed in the middle of Friday afternoon and stayed closed during the whole day of Saturday.
He went to the synagogue.
He put on the [NON-ENGLISH] every day.
They had the mezuzah on every single door.
And he also wear the kittel in the-- during the-- the high holy days.
And they always had the strictly kosher household, observing the Pesach, of course.
I really don't know, but-- but father and-- and grandfather felt that's how they want to do it, so they were doing it.
I really don't have any idea how other people did it.
Well, in Salgotarjan, in my time, there were not-- no Jewish schools.
However, we had the rabbi, who taught us liturgy and history, the Jewish law, and-- and every single thing.
But not in school.
We had free time given by the school when we had to observe the religious education.
But mostly at home.
It went all right for a while.
But, for example, my mother told me that one of her best friend married-- after the First World War-- an officer.
And when they came back after honeymoon to Salgotarjan and they met mother on the street-- they were very, very close friends-- she turned her head.
So mother understood, and she-- she reacted also in a way that the person became a non-person.
Oh, because she was Jewish, yes.
And the officer, of course, the Hungarian officer.
Not at the beginning.
Not at the beginning.

But gradually, like on Saturdays-- when I was still going to grammar school-- mother and grandfather used to pick me up, because it happen that the children-- and of course where do the children hear this?
At home-- started mocking Jewish girls.
We had a co-ed school system in Salgotarjan grammar school.

And then I went to the gymnasium which is something different from what we have here, because a gymnasium here is where you-- exercises and all that, but in Hungary it was the equivalent of junior and senior high school.
I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] attend four classes at the gymnasium, between age 10 and age 14.
Gradually, we felt that there is some pressure in the gymnasium on Jewish girls.
We were still not barred from that particular school, but my parents felt that I should follow my studies somewhere else.

Then it happened, in 1940, that a friend of my parents came up with the idea that there was a French convent in Budapest called Notre Dame de Sion, that's-- who-- which is running the equivalent of a high school, plus language and secretarial school, in Budapest.
And they would be more than willing to take new students.
They couldn't have less cared about religion, per se, because they didn't even ask.
We were not forced to 'convert,' quote, unquote-- nothing of that sort.
Religion didn't even come to-- to be a problem.
It was really an oasis during those times.
I was staying with friends in Budapest--
No.
No, not that.
My parents were not involved in Zionism.
We-- we-- they have-- I-- I remember there were-- there were meetings by the Keren Kayemeth, and there was another--
Keren Kayemeth.
Ah, Keren Kayemeth, the Jewish National Fund.
Jewish National Fund.
And, of course, they were-- they were contributors to that.
And then there-- they-- they had subscription to Zionist newspapers, but they were not active-- that's what I meant-- in the movement.
Yes.
At the beginning of the grammar school-- from between six and ten-- there really was not anything-- anything too bad about it.
But gradually, as the war broke out in-- the Second World War, in 1939.
I went to school.
And then my parents were rather strict about my homework, and I-- I tried to do my best.
I probably should have done much more than I did.
And I learned-- when I started at gymnasium I started also learning languages.
In the-- in the gymnasium that was also co-ed, we started Latin the first year.
And it was extremely difficult, and everybody hated it because of the grammar and all that.
And then when I took up French, I was very grateful for the four years in Latin, because it resembles.
And we had German.
I had German from home.
I started to talk German when I-- when I began to talk Hungarian.
And in-- in Budapest I started to study French.
But life in Salgotarjan was-- if I can say this-- it was easy for a child.
First of all, the loving background at home-- it was-- it was like paradise, all of these loving relatives at home, and my grandparents, and the aunts, and uncles, and the cousins.
And everybody rejoiced-- we rejoiced in each other as long as it lasted.
Father was in business with the two brothers so mother-- mother stayed home and attended to the house.
And as long as I-- as I lived with them, of course, supervised everything.
Schooling and whatever I was involved in she-- she knew about.
Mother was a marvelous friend.
Not only a mother-- she was my best friend.
I was very good friends with them.
I-- I really couldn't-- not even after all these years, there is absolutely nothing bad relating to the family that I could say.
It-- it-- it was really one of those exceptional good relations with-- with the family.
It was a-- You mean apartment-- room-wise?
It was-- it was in an apartment house not far from the factories.
And it was actually-- it-- it had a different layout, because when you-- when you walked in, you-- you were in a hall.
And then you had the bathroom to the-- I can still see it, you know-- to the right, and the kitchen, and the pantry.
In Hungary we had the pantry, not like a electric refrigerator like today.
We had the little icebox with the ice, and the iceman came and started knocking the ice off of the big, you know-- how do you call this in English?
The big-- Blocks.
Block of ice, yeah.
And then you went into the dining room.
And to the left was my mother and my father's bedroom.
To the right was my room.
And then we had the sitting room next to that.
It was a lovely apartment.
Except a few pieces of china, nothing.
Absolutely nothing.
But that would have been the least because tangible-- to lose tangible things doesn't mean a thing.
Nothing.
Eventually, in the late '30s, I believe, they-- Hungary was never a philo-Semitic country.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think there was something in the population that-- that's been always anti-Jewish.
I'm not saying everybody.
It would be very, very sad if everybody would be a villain in a nation of 10 millions.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] gradually, especially when the Second World War-- even before the World War in the late '30s-- they have enacted the first anti-Jewish decrees-- anti-Jewish legislation.
Government sponsored.
I believe that on the university level the numerus clausus-- which meant the quota system, what we call here-- probably was enacted in the late '30s.
And then, gradually, on the university level, it became the numerus nullus, which meant no Jews admitted.
I cannot remember the exact date.
But then when the Second World War broke out, on September 2, I believe, 1939, then, of course-- I must say of course-- Hungary sided with the Nazis.
I felt-- I felt this in school.
In 1939, I attended the first-- my first year at the gymnasium.

And I remember when the French were vanquished in the-- during spring-- I think it was in May or-- or April of 1940-- then one of my schoolmates-- I was in fourth grade and he was in eighth grade-- we are talking about the gymnasium-- he was extremely happy that the Germans were victorious over the French.
However, I have to add that after the war he went around in Salgotarjan saying that ask Olga Kovacs how I felt during the war.
And Olga Kovacs couldn't testify to that.
Hungary.
Yes.
To Budapest.
The decision was made, as I mentioned earlier, that the school-- Notre Dame de Sion-- accepted Jewish girls.
They did not even ask your religion.
All they wanted was to teach, and I felt immediately accepted.
And it-- it was like paradise.
Friends of ours.
Friends of ours-- who-- who advised my parents about the possibility of-- of-- or suggestion that we-- we should try that-- had a daughter there.
She-- she was a couple of years older than I.
She already started the secretarial school.
And the nuns were marvelous.
They were just really superb.
She was there.
I was staying with friends of ours in Budapest.
And I took the train-- it really was not the train.
It's the tramway or the bus that you took in-- in Hungary from the apartment to go to school.
It was not too far.
I mean, Budapest compared to New York-- it-- it's really not such a big city.
And I attended classes there.
And I-- I-- I had classes every day.
We had languages, history, literature-- everything.
It was marvelous.
